name: LITERARY NEW YORK CITY
clue: An insider described the scene there: "Just...loudmouths showing off, saving their gags for days, waiting to spring them\"

name: STATESMEN
clue: He abandoned plans for a military career at age 17 &, in 1607, became Bishop of Lucon

name: GERMAN AMERICANS
clue: He famously remarked, "We are all the President's men", giving Woodward & Bernstein their title

name: PULITZER PRIZE NOVELS
clue: The first Pulitzer Prize-winning novel by a woman, it became a movie in 1993

name: FEMALE SINGERS
clue: In December 2023 she became the oldest solo artist, at 78, to top the Billboard Hot 100 chart, with a song she recorded in 1958

name: WOMEN IN U.S. HISTORY
clue: In 1901 this activist was jailed for inspiring the assassination of William McKinley, but the charge was later dropped

name: HISTORIC QUOTATIONS
clue: According to Suetonius it was inscribed on a parade wagon after the 5-day Pontic campaign of 47 B.C.
response: \"Veni, Vidi, Vici" ("I came, I saw, I conquered")

name: SPORTS
clue: Its solo female winner is awarded the Venus Rosewater Dish

name: ELECTIONS
clue: Last third-party presidential candidate to carry any state

name: BOARD GAMES
clue: In Scrabble, the colors of the 2 squares that double & triple the word value

name: 19th CENTURY LITERARY CHARACTERS
clue: This character from an 1859 novel symbolizes the Fates, who in mythology spin the web of life, measure it & cut it off

name: 16th CENTURY NAMES
clue: As a foe of the Spanish, he's been called "The Queen's Pirate" & a "Gran Luterano\"

name: LANDMARKS
clue: Its creator said its parts represent our nation's founding, expansion, development & preservation

name: SHAKESPEARE'S TIME
clue: The line "a great reckoning in a little room" in "As You Like It" is usually taken to refer to this author's premature death

name: AIRPLANES
clue: This Dutch company whose founder made the Red Baron's triplane went bankrupt in 1996

name: ENTERTAINMENT AWARDS
clue: In 1998 she became the only woman to win an Oscar & an Emmy for Lead Actress in the same year

name: THE CALENDAR
clue: Calendar date with which the 20th century began

name: ZOOLOGY
clue: Animal species that's the subject of the longest consecutive study of any group of wild animals, 40 years

name: 19th CENTURY FICTION
clue: In Chapter 9 of an 1851 work, a preacher in a New England port city delivers a sermon about this Old Testament prophet

name: FAMOUS NAMES OF THE '50s
clue: It turned out to be the name of a childhood neighbor of hypnosis subject Virginia Tighe

name: PHRASES IN AMERICAN HISTORY
clue: This phrase linking 2 entities was in Jefferson's 1802 letter to the Danbury Baptists, elaborating on the 1st Amendment

name: PHYSICS TERMS
clue: Ironically, it's a metaphor meaning a huge step forward, but this 2-word process only occurs on a subatomic scale

name: THE 50 STATES
clue: Both in the Pacific, they are the 50 states' 2 biggest islands in area; one is about 40 degrees colder in winter than the other

name: MAGAZINES
clue: In 1925 it was founded by an ex-editor of Stars and Stripes; in the '90s it was run by an ex-editor of Vanity Fair

name: MILITARY SLANG
clue: This word for high-ranking officials comes from a metal used in 19th century military insignia

name: THE 1960s
clue: It was already a crime to alter one of these; a 1965 law passed 393-1 in the House criminalized burning one too

name: AUTOMOTIVE HISTORY
clue: This Ford with a name from Native American myth was the first model to be Motor Trend Car of the Year

name: ARTISTS
clue: The February 17, 1901 death of his friend Carles Casagemas made this grief-stricken artist change his color palette

name: LITERARY QUOTES
clue: A maxim of Ayn Rand was "Man's ego is" this "of human progress\"

name: GOVERNMENT PROGRAMS
clue: Over 8,000 people have been saved from harm by this program authorized in a 1970 law, but we're not allowed to name any

name: FRENCH PAINTERS
clue: This French painter wrote, "I am good for nothing except painting and gardening\"

name: MONARCHS
clue: In her will, she wrote of her hope of just rule for the Indians of the New World

name: ISLANDS
clue: This isolated Pacific island 1400 miles SE of Tahiti is named for the first European who sighted it, in 1767

name: WORLD LEADERS
clue: The first prime minister of his country to be born in that land, he was assassinated in 1995

name: SYMBOLS
clue: One legend says Clovis, king of the Franks, adopted this symbol after flowers revealed a safe river crossing for his army

name: 20th CENTURY TECHNOLOGY
clue: The first major use of simultaneous translation, before adoption by the U.N., was in this European city in 1945 & 1946

name: THE 5 W's
clue: The names of the president & premier of the world's most populous nation are homophones of these 2 words

name: ON VACATION IN ITALY
clue: About 30 miles from Florence, a little hill gives this tiny Tuscan town its name, familiar to American visitors

name: FRENCH CLASSICAL MUSIC
clue: This 1928 work repeats a theme, almost entirely in C major, in an unvarying rhythm & has a crescendo lasting 17 minutes

name: MISS AMERICA
clue: He replaced Bert Parks as host of Miss America pageant for 1980

name: U.S. PRESIDENTS
clue: He was the 1st Democrat elected president after the Civil War

name: THEATER
clue: In November 1864 John Wilkes Booth & his brothers were fittingly part of a performance of this Shakespeare play

name: CONTEMPORARY AUTHORS
clue: At the start of his writing career, his wife told him, if it didn't work out, at 6'4", he could be a reacher in a supermarket

name: ORGANIZATIONS
clue: It was founded in 1938 as the National Foundation for Infantile Paralysis

name: BROADWAY
clue: Following a show's success in 2011, this group began advertising, "You've seen the play...now read the book\"
response: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

name: CURRENT TELEVISION
clue: George Romero declined to direct a few episodes of this series, calling it "basically... just a soap opera\"

name: THE INTERNET
clue: Among 2-letter country codes in Internet addresses, "de" is Germany, "kh" is Cambodia & "es" is this country

name: THE SUPREME COURT
clue: He served as chief justice for more of the 20th century than any other man—17 years

name: HISTORIC AMERICANS
clue: He turned down an appointment as a U.S. senator in 1875 because it meant accepting a pardon for treason

name: WORLD AIRPORTS
clue: This city's international airport opened in 1942, just in time to be the destination of a flight at the end of a movie

name: CLASSIC LITERATURE
clue: Chapter 1 of this book describes "a cyclone cellar, where the family could go in case one of those... whirlwinds arose\"

name: FRENCH FOOD HISTORY
clue: A popular product was born when Jean Naigeon of this city substituted the juice of unripe grapes for vinegar

name: 18th CENTURY SCOTSMEN
clue: A relative of this inventor described him as a boy staring at the tea kettle for an hour watching it boil

name: COUNTRY NAMES
clue: In England in 1933, Choudhry Rahmat Ali coined this name, a country that wouldn't be formed until 14 years later

name: HISTORIC NAMES
clue: In 1895 he founded the Wyoming town now considered the eastern entrance to Yellowstone

name: U.S. HISTORY
clue: Messrs. Gusenberg, Gusenberg, May, Weinshank, Clark, Heyer & Schwimmer famously died on this day in 1929

name: SOUTH AMERICA
clue: The name of this capital city is a corruption of Rimac, the river on which it lies

name: BIBLICAL AKA
clue: This second king of Israel was "the sweet singer of Israel\"

name: INTERNATIONAL BORDERS
clue: 2 of the 3 countries that share land borders with Russia & China
response: (2 of) Kazakhstan, Mongolia, or North Korea

name: COLLEGES & UNIVERSITIES
clue: The only state that's home to 2 Ivy League schools
response: New York (Columbia, in NYC & Cornell, in Ithaca)

name: BRITISH ROYALTY
clue: The only British monarch of the 20th century not to produce an heir

name: FROM THE DESK OF THE POPE
clue: A 1919 letter quotes Jesus, "Go into the whole world and preach the gospel" & notes the vigilance, energy & hardships of these workers

name: THE 1850s
clue: He published a book of prose & verse quotes "to trace to their source passages and phrases in common use\"

name: HISTORIC NAMES
clue: For his licentious behavior, monk Grigori Yefimovich Novykh earned this nickname meaning "debauched one\"

name: SYMBOLIC OBJECTS
clue: To complete one of its regular trips, in 1948 it took a boat across the English Channel; in 1952 it took a plane en route to Finland

name: ART
clue: The Italian name of this 15th century masterpiece is "L'Ultima Cena\"

name: THE SPACE SHUTTLE
clue: 2 of the 3 states where astronauts have landed the Space Shuttle

name: FILMS OF THE 1950s
clue: The action in this film begins at 10:30 A.M. & plays out in almost-real time until 12:15

name: BORDERS
clue: Twice as long as Hadrian's Wall, Offa's Dyke was the traditional border between these 2 lands

name: DOING BUSINESS INTERNATIONALLY
clue: This U.S. retailer's 133 Canadian stores closed in 2015; its CEO said, "We missed the mark... by taking on too much too fast\"

name: 20th CENTURY ART
clue: In occupied Paris, a German officer asked Picasso if he had done this masterpiece; he replied, "No, you did\"

name: TRAVEL EUROPE
clue: (Sarah of the Clue Crew gives the clue while aboard the Orient Express.) Today, the Orient Express' Paris-Istanbul run crosses 5 countries between France & Turkey; these are 3 of them
response: (3 of) Switzerland, Austria, Hungary, Romania & Bulgaria

name: THE SOLAR SYSTEM
clue: This planet's moons include Triton & the recently discovered Naiad

name: BRASS INSTRUMENTS
clue: In playing this instrument whose early version was called a sackbut, it's about 6" from A to B, about 7" from C to D

name: THE CIVIL WAR
clue: The bloodiest single day of fighting in the Civil War took place in this state

name: EUROPEAN AUTHORS
clue: The name of this author who died in 1924 has become an adjective meaning surreal or nightmarish

name: HOLIDAY SONGS
clue: This song had its beginnings as a book handed out to children at Christmas at Montgomery Ward

name: BRITISH PLACES
clue: This city owes much of its early history to a temple dedicated to Sulis Minerva & a "sacred spring" found there

name: REPUBLICANS
clue: 2 of the 3 Republicans elected vice president twice
response: (2 of) Spiro Agnew, George (H.W.) Bush & Richard Nixon

name: LITERARY FIREARMS
clue: The "Polizei Pistole Kurz" model was often used very effectively by this literary character introduced in 1953

name: NOVEL TITLES
clue: The title of this 1951 novel comes from the hero's fantasy of rescuing children falling from a cliff

name: NAMES IN THE NEWS
clue: This World Chess champ is Deputy Chairman of the new Democratic Party of Russia

name: FAMOUS NAMES
clue: At his death in January 2010, he was called "the Garbo of letters, famous for not wanting to be famous\"

name: COLLEGE TOWNS
clue: 2 schools in the Southeastern Conference are located in cities with this same name but in different states

name: CABINET DEPARTMENTS
clue: \"Si ve algo, diga algo" was part of a 2011 Spanish-language TV campaign by this Cabinet department

name: SONG CLASSICS
clue: In a song from the '30s musical "Roberta" this line follows "When a lovely flame dies...\"

name: MOVIE SETTINGS
clue: In 2017 this New York City luxury store opened its first cafe, with truffle eggs, waffles & croissants on the menu

name: U.S. CITIES
clue: The seal of this historic New England city has the phrase "What a glorious morning for America" & the date "April 19\"

name: SHAKESPEAREAN REFERENCES
clue: This name given to U.K. labor strife in December 1978 & January 1979 was taken from the first line of a Shakespeare history play

name: WORD ORIGINS
clue: This residential district of Dublin held an annual fair from 1204 to 1855, when it was closed due to frequent fights

name: NONFICTION
clue: In 1947 editor William Styron said this book was a long, "tedious Pacific voyage best suited" for National Geographic

name: WORD HISTORIES
clue: In old philosophy this 12-letter word referred to a fifth substance, superior to earth, air, fire or water

name: AGRICULTURE
clue: The Norse were impressed by these in North America, but they weren't grown profitably in North America until around 1850

name: ACTOR-DIRECTORS
clue: The only woman to win a Golden Globe for directing, she won for a 1983 film that she had also co-written

name: SHAKESPEARE
clue: Samuel Johnson said Shakespeare "so carefully informs us" that this play is set on the eve of May Day & yet called it this

name: VICE PRESIDENTS
clue: Of the 5 former vice presidents still living, he held the office the earliest

name: WORLD RELIGION
clue: This country has the most Protestants of any in the world

name: MONARCHIES
clue: 1 of the 2 largely German-speaking monarchies in the world; they're about 250 miles apart

name: TV ANIMATION
clue: This teen duo debuted in a 1992 animated short in which they played baseball with a frog

name: THE TOY BOX
clue: Spud & Yam are 2 of the offspring of this toy introduced in 1952
